skill.name: cc-skill-clickhouse-io
skill.description: ClickHouse guidance for schema design, query optimization, materialized views, ingestion patterns, monitoring, and analytical SQL workflows.
skill.summary: Apply practical ClickHouse patterns for fast analytical workloads.
skill.author: affaan-m

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives caused by markdown code fences, ClickHouse SQL examples, and normal environment-variable examples. The confirmed issue is unsafe sample TypeScript that builds SQL with direct string interpolation, which could teach SQL injection-prone ingestion code if copied without parameterization or escaping.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe SQL String Interpolation in Examples
medium_findings.description: The TypeScript bulk insert example constructs SQL values by interpolating trade fields directly into an INSERT statement. If copied into production with untrusted data, this pattern can allow SQL injection or malformed inserts; use ClickHouse client structured inserts, parameterized input, or strict escaping instead.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly concatenates values from trade fields into SQL text. The file is documentation rather than executable skill code, so the risk is unsafe guidance rather than direct runtime compromise.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Trigger Command Execution Alerts
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many markdown backtick fences as Ruby or shell execution. These locations are fenced SQL and TypeScript examples in documentation, not executable skill runtime code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review shows markdown code blocks and query examples, not Ruby backtick operators or shell command execution. No executable wrapper or script file exists in the analyzed file set.

low_findings.title: Environment Variables Used for Database Credentials
low_findings.description: The examples read ClickHouse and PostgreSQL connection settings from environment variables. This is a standard configuration pattern, but users should avoid logging these values or passing them to untrusted endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variables are only shown as local database client configuration. I found no evidence of network exfiltration, logging of secrets, or hidden credential collection.

low_findings.title: Static Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: Weak-crypto and reconnaissance alerts map to ClickHouse SQL terms, markdown text, and system table monitoring examples. The system.query_log and system.parts queries are legitimate database observability guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines inspect ClickHouse metadata tables and discuss performance monitoring. I found no evidence of host reconnaissance, cryptographic implementation, malware behavior, or prompt injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copyable SQL Injection-Prone Insert Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample ingestion code assembles an INSERT statement with template literals and field values. This should be replaced with structured inserts or properly escaped values before use with untrusted data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The interpolation pattern is explicit and appears in a recommended bulk insert section. The impact is guidance-level because the marketplace skill itself does not execute the sample.

content.value_statement: ClickHouse schemas and queries can be hard to tune for large analytics workloads. This skill gives practical patterns for tables, inserts, materialized views, monitoring, and analytics queries.
actual_capabilities: Explains common ClickHouse table engines, including MergeTree, ReplacingMergeTree, and AggregatingMergeTree.
Shows query patterns for filtering, aggregation, window functions, funnels, cohorts, and retention analysis.
Describes batch, streaming, ETL, and CDC ingestion approaches for analytics pipelines.
Provides materialized view patterns for real-time aggregate tables.
Includes monitoring queries for slow queries, table size, merge activity, and storage usage.
Summarizes practical design rules for partitions, ordering keys, data types, and batching.
limitations: It is a documentation skill and does not connect to a live ClickHouse server.
Example schemas use generic market and event data and require adaptation.
Some TypeScript examples need safer escaping or structured inserts before production use.
It does not benchmark queries against your actual data distribution.

use_cases.title: Design an Analytics Schema
use_cases.description: Create a ClickHouse table layout that matches common filters, time ranges, and aggregation needs.

use_cases.title: Tune Slow Analytical Queries
use_cases.description: Review query shape, ordering keys, aggregation functions, and monitoring data to reduce query cost.

use_cases.title: Plan Real-Time Metrics Pipelines
use_cases.description: Use materialized views and batch ingestion patterns to prepare dashboards and hourly aggregates.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Table Engine
prompt_templates.prompt: I am storing analytics events in ClickHouse. Compare MergeTree, ReplacingMergeTree, and AggregatingMergeTree for my use case and recommend one.

prompt_templates.title: Review an Ordering Key
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this ClickHouse table design and suggest partition and ordering key changes for queries filtered by date, account, and event type.

prompt_templates.title: Improve an Analytics Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this slow ClickHouse query and suggest changes to filters, aggregation functions, selected columns, and materialized views.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Ingestion Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a ClickHouse ingestion plan for high-volume events from PostgreSQL CDC, including batching, deduplication, materialized views, and monitoring.

output_examples.input: A user asks how to store event analytics with deduplication needs.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends a ReplacingMergeTree pattern, explains the ordering key tradeoffs, and notes how deduplication affects query freshness.

output_examples.input: A user asks why a dashboard query is slow over recent events.
output_examples.output: The skill reviews indexed filter order, selected columns, aggregation functions, and monitoring tables to identify likely bottlenecks.

output_examples.input: A user asks how to maintain hourly metrics for dashboards.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines a materialized view approach with aggregate state columns and merge functions for efficient reads.

best_practices: Design tables around the most common query filters and time ranges.
Batch inserts and use structured client APIs when loading untrusted data.
Monitor query duration, read volume, memory use, table size, and merge operations.
anti_patterns: Do not copy string-interpolated SQL examples into production without escaping or structured inserts.
Do not rely on SELECT * for large analytical queries.
Do not create many tiny partitions or frequent single-row inserts.

faq.question: Does this skill run ClickHouse queries for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and examples, but it does not connect to a database or execute queries.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is this suitable for production schema design?
faq.answer: It can guide design discussions, but you should test every schema and query with real data.

faq.question: Why is the security risk marked medium?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation-only, but some insertion examples use direct SQL string interpolation that should not be copied unchanged.

faq.question: Does it include ClickHouse monitoring guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for slow query review, table sizes, disk usage, and merge operations.

faq.question: Does it handle streaming or CDC pipelines?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes streaming inserts, ETL patterns, and PostgreSQL change notification examples.
